original_hypothesis.title: Incomplete Checkpoint Loop Corrupts Global Voting Power After Extended Inactivity
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The _checkpoint function iterates a maximum of 255 weeks to fill the global point history. Since locks last 260 weeks (5 years), if the contract is inactive for more than 255 weeks, the loop terminates early. The next user action will add their current slope/bias to a stale global point from the past, permanently corrupting the global voting power math.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Loop bound is strictly less than the maximum possible time horizon of active state (255 weeks vs 260 weeks), guaranteeing state desynchronization if the gap is hit.

proof_of_signal.pattern: for (uint256 i = 0; i < 255; i++) loop bound is less than LOCKTIME (260 weeks)

preconditions: No state-changing actions occur on VotingEscrow for > 255 weeks

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Loop breaks at 255 weeks, but global point is updated with current block timestamp's user bias
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Adding a present-day user bias to a 5-week-old global point corrupts the linear decay math
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Loop bound should be at least 261 to cover the full LOCKTIME

analysis_questions: Does the math corruption allow users to claim more voting power than they should have?
Can the state be repaired by subsequent checkpoints?
validation_requirements: Call createLock and verify global pointHistory math is corrupted

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: After 4.9 years of inactivity on a specific deployment, a user creates a new lock. The checkpoint loop processes 255 weeks and stops. The user's new voting power is added to the stale global state, resulting in a permanently inflated or corrupted global totalSupply.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Corruption of global voting power, affecting gauge weight distributions

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: Missing long-term time-travel tests exceeding 255 weeks

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible as sudden jumps in totalSupply

validation_result.mechanism: insufficient loop bound vs lock duration
validation_result.impact_summary: The _checkpoint loop bound of 255 weeks is strictly less than the LOCKTIME of ~260 weeks. If the contract is inactive for >255 weeks, the loop cannot process all pending slope changes, corrupting global voting power state.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The VotingEscrow._checkpoint function uses a loop bounded at 255 iterations, each representing one week. The LOCKTIME constant is 1825 days, which equals ~260.71 weeks. When a user creates a lock, a slope change is scheduled at their lock end time, which could be up to 260 weeks in the future (after floor-to-week rounding). If no state-changing transaction occurs on the contract for more than 255 weeks, the loop in _checkpoint cannot iterate enough times to reach block.timestamp. The loop will exhaust its 255 iterations, exit without hitting the `break` statement, and leave lastPoint with a stale timestamp and block number. Any subsequent user action will add their current slope/bias to this stale global point, and slope changes in weeks 256-260 remain unprocessed. The same 255-iteration limit exists in _supplyAt, meaning totalSupply calculations would also be incorrect. The code comment on line 169 explicitly acknowledges this risk: 'Hopefully it won't happen that this won't get used in 5 years! If it does, users will be able to withdraw but vote weight will be broken.' This confirms the developers recognized the limitation but did not fix it. The loop bound should be at least 261 to cover the full LOCKTIME range.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Lines 168-196 of VotingEscrow.sol clearly show the loop bound of 255. Line 34 shows LOCKTIME = 1825 days. 1825 / 7 = 260.71 weeks, so 255 < ceil(260.71) = 261. The gap is provably present in the code. The same issue exists in _supplyAt (lines 367-383) with an identical 255-iteration bound.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Loop iterates at most 255 times (255 weeks), but LOCKTIME = 1825 days ≈ 260.71 weeks. If no checkpoint occurs for >255 weeks, the loop cannot reach block.timestamp. It exits without break, leaving lastPoint.ts stale at 255 weeks past the last checkpoint instead of block.timestamp. Subsequent code then adds user slope/bias to this stale point and stores it in pointHistory[epoch].
Same 255-week limit applies to _supplyAt, which is used by totalSupply() and totalSupplyAt(). If the point to supply-at gap exceeds 255 weeks, totalSupply returns incorrect values.
1825 days / 7 days = 260.71 weeks. This exceeds the 255-week loop bound by approximately 5.71 weeks.

calling_sequence: 1. No transactions for 256+ weeks since last checkpoint
6. Loop runs 255 iterations (i=0..254), advancing 255 weeks from lastCheckpoint (line 168)
7. block.timestamp is 256+ weeks ahead, so iterativeTime never reaches block.timestamp
8. Loop exits without break; lastPoint.ts = lastCheckpoint + 255*WEEK (not block.timestamp)
9. User's new slope/bias added to stale lastPoint (lines 199-206)
11. Slope changes from weeks 256-260 are never applied, permanently lost
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: When the for loop at line 168 runs all 255 iterations without iterativeTime reaching block.timestamp, the loop exits naturally (i reaches 255). The lastPoint retains ts from 255 weeks past the last checkpoint. At line 199-206, the new user's slope and bias (calculated relative to block.timestamp) are added to this stale global point. The resulting pointHistory entry has inconsistent time (stale ts) with user-contributed values (current time bias). Furthermore, any slopeChanges scheduled in weeks 256-260 are never processed, permanently skewing the global slope.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment configuration affects the loop bound (255) or LOCKTIME (1825 days). These are hardcoded constants.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario requires >255 weeks (~4.9 years) of complete contract inactivity. While unlikely on an active chain, it is possible on low-activity deployments. After this inactivity period, the next createLock or checkpoint call will: (1) fail to process slope changes from weeks 256-260, (2) store a global point with stale timestamp, (3) add the new user's bias/slope to a point that doesn't reflect the actual current global state. This permanently corrupts totalSupply calculations and voting power accounting. The corruption cannot be repaired by subsequent checkpoints because the missed slope changes are skipped over entirely.
validation_result.impact_assessment: If triggered, the global voting power history becomes permanently corrupted. The totalSupply function will return incorrect values, affecting any governance decisions or gauge weight calculations that depend on it. The corruption is permanent because slope changes from the missed 5-week window are never applied. While the precondition of 255+ weeks of inactivity is unlikely for active protocols, the code defect is real: the loop bound (255) is provably insufficient for the lock duration (260 weeks).
recommended_mitigations: Increase the loop bound from 255 to at least 261 (ceil(1825/7) + 1) in both _checkpoint and _supplyAt
Consider adding a safety margin: use 270 or higher to account for any edge cases in week-floor rounding
Add a require or assertion that verifies iterativeTime reached block.timestamp after the loop exits in _checkpoint
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy VotingEscrow in a Foundry test. 2. Have a user createLock with some CANTO. 3. Use vm.warp to advance time by 256 weeks. 4. Call checkpoint() or have another user createLock. 5. Verify that pointHistory[globalEpoch].ts != block.timestamp (it should be ~1 week behind). 6. Verify totalSupply() returns an incorrect value compared to the expected decayed sum of all user biases.
uncertainties: The practical likelihood of 255+ weeks of complete contract inactivity is very low on active chains
Multiple checkpoint calls could iteratively catch up 255 weeks at a time, but this requires someone to know the state is stale and call checkpoint repeatedly
additional_testing_needed: Test with exactly 255, 256, and 260 weeks of inactivity to confirm the boundary behavior
Test whether repeated checkpoint() calls can incrementally catch up beyond 255 weeks
Test the interaction between corrupted global state and GaugeController voting weight calculations
